title: TBM 323: Quick Tool Tips
questions: How can we make roadmapping and goal-tracking tools work for our team instead of becoming zombie process?
What stakeholders should we involve when choosing and customizing strategy deployment tools?
Should we automate immediately or validate with low-fi prototypes first?
When do we scrap our tool/process and start over?
summary: The post offers practical guidance for teams selecting, adopting, and customizing tools for roadmapping, goal setting/tracking, and strategy deployment. It’s aimed at product and cross-functional leaders who want to avoid zombie process and improve adoption by treating tools like internal products—validated with low-fi experiments, informed by multiple perspectives, and integrated into real rituals and incentives.

title: TBM 311: Roadmapping Miro Board and Question for Subscribers
questions: Should a product/engineering newsletter be split into separate tracks for actionable tactics versus existential/organizational topics?
How do you prevent content recommendations from feeling like 'word salad' to readers who want practical guidance?
What is the best way to write for a highly diverse product development audience with different mental models and interests?
search_terms: organizational design and politics at work
how to write for diverse audiences
summary: The post shares a Miro board resource for facilitating team roadmapping discussions and asks readers for input on newsletter direction. It argues that mixing tactical, actionable posts with dense existential/mental-model content can polarize readers in a diverse product development audience. The author considers splitting the newsletter into two tracks—one for tools and step-by-step advice and one for macro-level organizational and individual challenges.

title: Plan on a Page Checklist
questions: What is our mission and high-level focus right now?
What is going well and what isn’t, and what is the key leverage point?
What should we accomplish in the next 3 months and 12 months?
What are our workstreams, initiatives, milestones, dependencies, non-goals, assumptions, and risks?
summary: This post provides a practical checklist for creating a “plan on a page” that aligns teams around mission, a clear diagnosis, time-bounded focus, and the workstreams that drive outcomes. It’s aimed at leaders and operators who need a lightweight but complete planning artifact, including a 12-month roadmap, milestones, dependencies, non-goals, assumptions, and risks—while avoiding premature commitment.

title: TBM 46/52: Annual Planning (Wicked Problem)
questions: Why can’t product teams do more continuous planning instead of annual planning?
What makes annual planning a wicked problem in real organizations?
How do execution mode, feedback latency, and output-centricity undermine strategy work?
What small experiments could replace the annual planning ritual with more frequent planning cycles?
search_terms: reduce feedback loop latency product planning
summary: The post argues that while continuous planning is theoretically achievable and beneficial, annual planning persists because it functions like a wicked problem shaped by organizational constraints. It helps product and strategy leaders understand the real-world drivers—execution mode, slow feedback, output pressure, budgeting and executive timing—that make frequent planning difficult, and suggests experimenting with recurring planning time blocks.

title: TBM 41b/52: Suitably Detailed Roadmap Items
questions: How do you structure a one-year rolling product roadmap with the right level of detail at each time horizon?
Which metrics best capture progress for accountants’ monthly reconciliation workflows?
What roadmap initiatives (model improvements, UX, education, collaboration) most effectively reduce reconciliation time and errors?
How should teams prioritize among multiple potential options for near-future workflow improvements?
summary: The post outlines how to build a one-year rolling product roadmap for a specific user group (Tier 3 accountants) using progressive disclosure: high-level intent for far-future work, measurable targets for near-future work, and prioritized options for immediate execution. It emphasizes outcome metrics such as 90th percentile reconciliation time and proposes initiatives spanning AI categorization improvements, UX changes for scanning and clarification, and organizational research on collaboration patterns.

title: TBM 7/52: Risk Checklist Activity
questions: How can our team talk about risk and uncertainty in a way that leads to better planning?
What initiative risks are we missing when our risk section is only a few generic bullets?
How do we ensure our risk checklist is specific, actionable, and not just theater?
How can we validate and improve a risk checklist using past projects?
search_terms: pressure test risk questions with historical efforts
summary: The post proposes a practical, team-run activity for creating and using a checklist of initiative risk questions to improve how teams discuss uncertainty. It is aimed at product and project teams that currently treat risk as a one-time PRD section or focus narrowly on schedule risks. The guidance emphasizes specificity, cross-functional input, historical validation, continuous revision, and avoiding performative review dynamics.

title: TBM 3/52: Help Your Team Find Its Own Way
questions: Why does a six-week planning/batch cycle work for some teams, and what principles make it effective in context?
How do we balance set-up costs and the time spent working when choosing a cadence?
What does it mean to copy another company’s process without losing what makes it work?
How can we assess whether a practice improves decision quality and decision flow?
search_terms: lean planning inventory zero backlog roadmap
summary: The post argues that product development cadences (e.g., six-week cycles) succeed only when the underlying principles and incentives are understood in context. It’s aimed at product and engineering leaders who want to learn from other companies without copying them blindly, using a checklist mindset focused on decision quality and decision flow.

title: TBM 2.1/52: Continuous Roadmapping
questions: How can we avoid the waste caused by rushed, big-batch roadmap planning?
What does continuous roadmapping look like in practice for product teams?
How do we keep a roadmap always up to date without overplanning or estimation?
What should replace feature-count roadmaps when releases are frequent?
search_terms: roadmap as a set of bets
summary: The post argues that traditional big-batch roadmap planning and reactive whack-a-mole prioritization both waste money and attention. It proposes continuous roadmapping using a small, always-filled board of time-horizon “bets,” grounded in a north star metric, persistent goals, and outcome-oriented one-pagers. It’s aimed at product leaders and agile teams who want an always up-to-date roadmap without overcommitting or relying on heavy estimation.

title: TBM 48/53: One Roadmap
questions: Should we split customer-facing and internal/infrastructure work into separate roadmaps?
How do we get executives to understand and prioritize unsexy infrastructure work?
What causes shadow roadmaps and how can we prevent them?
How can engineering leaders build a strong business case for enabling work?
search_terms: how to present infrastructure roadmap to executives
summary: The post argues for maintaining a single, integrated roadmap rather than splitting customer-facing and non-customer-facing work. It’s aimed at product and engineering leaders who present roadmaps to executives and want infrastructure/enabling work to be valued, prioritized, and understood. The core message is that visibility and business-case communication prevent shadow roadmaps, reduce debt buildup, and protect trust.

title: TBM 35/53: Basic Prioritization Questions (and When to Converge on a Solution)
questions: How much solutioning should we do before starting an effort?
Which uncertainties matter most right now, and how confident are we about each?
When should we experiment to de-risk instead of settling on a solution?
What happens to risk if we converge on a solution early?
search_terms: when to converge on a solution
summary: This post argues that product teams should delay solutioning until they’ve clarified key uncertainties, especially opportunity value and opportunity size. It offers a practical set of prioritization questions and confidence-based reasoning to decide when to experiment versus when to converge on a solution. It’s aimed at product managers, designers, and engineers who want better alignment and lower risk in early product development.

title: TBM 21/53: "Vision" and Prescriptive Roadmaps
questions: How do you communicate product vision so people trust it and feel commitment?
Why do feature factories persist even when teams do good work?
What does it mean to sell outcomes instead of selling future features?
How can an outcome-oriented roadmap avoid getting reset by leadership overcorrection?
summary: The post argues that “product vision” often functions as a trust and commitment signal, so many organizations respond better to prescriptive roadmaps that clearly state what will be built. It explains how incentives and organizational optics can entrench feature-factory behavior, and why outcome-focused teams may still struggle without actively selling outcomes and broadcasting real results. It’s aimed at product leaders and product managers trying to shift from feature-centric roadmaps to outcome-oriented planning without losing organizational buy-in.
